formations_blocks: Côte d’Ivoire slowing the tempo after an early fast start
key_matchups: Enner Valencia vs the Côte d’Ivoire defense around Agbadou
phases.summary: The opening phase is played at a high tempo, with Ecuador generating the more notable shooting opportunities while Côte d’Ivoire settle into a slower rhythm after the initial burst. The commentary suggests Ecuador are pressing enough to unsettle the Ivorians, who are also being sloppy in possession and not stringing many passes together. Despite the chances, Ecuador’s finishing is poor, with several efforts going well over or wide rather than testing the goalkeeper.

formations_blocks: Ecuador pressing and counter-pressing after turnovers
Côte d’Ivoire defending in a compact shape and leaving Ecuador chasing the ball
Ecuador building through quick restarts and recycled possession
adjustments: Pépé choosing to drive centrally rather than release wide to Diomande
Ecuador taking a free kick backwards, then playing quickly upfield to exploit the moment
Diomande stopping, pausing, then beating his man with a single touch to win a corner
key_matchups: Pépé vs Diomande on the right/inside channel
phases.summary: This phase was open and transitional, with Ecuador repeatedly recovering second balls and turning possession into immediate shots, while Côte d’Ivoire looked dangerous when they could attack directly after turnovers. The game featured several isolated duels and individual actions, including Diomande’s quick-feet break, Pépé’s central dribble, and Ecuador’s repeated attempts that twice struck the bar.

The interval arrives with Côte d’Ivoire and Ecuador locked at 0-0, and the commentary offers little in the way of concrete tactical detail from the first half itself. The main takeaway is contextual: both sides are being discussed as capable of progressing, and notably they are not appearing to play cautiously for a draw despite the tournament format incentives. Côte d’Ivoire are also framed as an underrated attacking side, with Yan Diomande singled out as a player who can trouble defenses.

adjustments: Côte d’Ivoire used Yahia Fofana to spring counters after Ecuador pressure
Diamonde moved into the center around the 50' mark
Ecuador slowed the tempo after being exposed to Ivorian counterattacks
Ecuador substituted Nilson Angulo for Minda
Côte d’Ivoire substituted Amad Diallo and Ange-Yoan Bonny for Wahi and Touré
key_matchups: Wahi vs the Ecuador goal frame on the 52' counter chance
Singo vs the cleared corner delivery on the bicycle-kick attempt
Diamonde operating on the right, drawing attention in transition
phases.summary: This phase was defined by swings between Ecuador’s possession and Côte d’Ivoire’s direct counters, with both teams creating danger mainly from transitional moments rather than sustained control. Côte d’Ivoire repeatedly threatened quickly after regains, prompting Ecuador to become more cautious in possession and slow the game down, while substitutions on both sides added fresh attacking legs without fundamentally changing the open, chance-heavy pattern.

adjustments: Côte d’Ivoire: Oulaï and Sangaré on for Seko Fofana and Pépé
Ecuador: Rodríguez on for Enner Valencia
Côte d’Ivoire: Kossounou on for Doué after a risky yellow-card situation
Ecuador leaning on wide delivery/crosses and a late free kick/corner sequence
key_matchups: Diallo vs trio of Ecuadorian defenders
Diamonde vs Ecuadorian defenders on the left
phases.summary: The closing phase was cagey at first, with both sides appearing content to play patiently and avoid overcommitting until a late goal might decide it. Ecuador generated their best threat through left-sided dribbling and dead-ball pressure, but Côte d’Ivoire survived the dangerous moments, made a defensive substitution after Doué flirted with a second yellow, and then snatched the winner through Diallo in the 90th minute. Ecuador looked stunned afterward and nearly lost structure immediately after conceding.
